sections.title: The Nominative Case
sections.explanation: German has four grammatical cases that show the role of a noun in a sentence. The nominative case marks the subject — the person or thing performing the action. In the nominative, the articles are: der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter), die (plural) — these are the base forms you already know. The indefinite articles are: ein (masculine), eine (feminine), ein (neuter). Examples: Der Mann lacht. (The man laughs.) — 'Der Mann' is the subject (nominative). Eine Frau singt. (A woman sings.) — 'Eine Frau' is the subject.
sections.tip: Nominative = the subject. Nothing changes from the basic article forms you already know: der/die/das, ein/eine/ein.

exercises.prompt: Which case marks the subject of a sentence?
exercises.explanation: The nominative case marks the subject — the person or thing performing the action of the verb.

exercises.prompt: In 'Der Mann lacht', what case is 'der Mann'?
exercises.explanation: 'Der Mann' is the subject of 'lachen' (to laugh) — it performs the action. Therefore it is in the nominative case.

exercises.prompt: ___ Katze schläft. (The cat sleeps.) Use the nominative article.
exercises.hint: Katze is feminine — use the nominative feminine article
exercises.explanation: 'Katze' is feminine → nominative article is 'die'. Die Katze schläft.

exercises.prompt: What is the nominative indefinite article for a neuter noun like 'Haus'?
exercises.explanation: Neuter nominative indefinite article is 'ein': ein Haus (a house). Masculine also uses 'ein'; only feminine uses 'eine'.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun to its nominative article (definite):
exercises.explanation: Nominative definite: der (masc.), die (fem.), das (neut.), die (pl.). These are the base forms.

sections.title: The Accusative Case
sections.explanation: The accusative case marks the direct object — the person or thing receiving the action directly. In the accusative, ONLY the masculine article changes: 'der' becomes 'den', and 'ein' becomes 'einen'. Feminine, neuter, and plural articles stay the same. This is the only change in the accusative! Article table — Definite: den (masc.), die (fem.), das (neut.), die (pl.). Indefinite: einen (masc.), eine (fem.), ein (neut.), no article (pl.). Example: 'Ich sehe den Mann.' (I see the man.) — 'den Mann' is the direct object (accusative masculine).
sections.tip: Only masculine changes in the accusative: der → den, ein → einen. All other genders stay the same!

examples.en: I see the man. (Mann = direct object → acc. masc. → den)
She buys a dog. (Hund = direct object → acc. masc. → einen)
He reads the newspaper. (feminine — no change: die)
We eat the bread. (neuter — no change: das)

exercises.prompt: In German accusative case, which gender's article changes?
exercises.explanation: Only masculine articles change in the accusative: der→den and ein→einen. Feminine (die/eine), neuter (das/ein), and plural (die) stay the same.

exercises.prompt: What is the accusative form of 'der Mann'?
exercises.explanation: Masculine 'der' becomes 'den' in the accusative. 'Ich sehe den Mann' = I see the man.

exercises.prompt: Ich kaufe ___ Hund. (I buy a dog.) — Hund is masculine
exercises.explanation: Masculine indefinite accusative: ein → einen. Ich kaufe einen Hund.

exercises.prompt: What is the accusative of 'eine Frau' (a woman)?
exercises.explanation: Feminine articles do NOT change in the accusative. 'eine Frau' stays 'eine Frau' as a direct object.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun phrase to its accusative form:
exercises.explanation: Only masculine changes: der→den, ein→einen. Feminine (die/eine) and neuter (das/ein) stay the same.
'Apfel' (apple) is masculine. As the direct object (accusative), the indefinite article ein→einen: 'Er kauft einen Apfel'.

exercises.prompt: Ich sehe ___ Frau. (I see the woman.) — definite, feminine
exercises.hint: Feminine definite article — no change in accusative
exercises.explanation: Feminine 'die' stays 'die' in the accusative. 'Ich sehe die Frau.'

exercises.prompt: What is the accusative of 'das Buch' (the book)?
exercises.explanation: Neuter articles do not change in the accusative. 'das Buch' stays 'das Buch': Ich lese das Buch (I read the book).

exercises.prompt: Identify the accusative object in: 'Die Frau kauft einen Rock.'
exercises.explanation: 'Die Frau' is the subject (nominative). 'einen Rock' (a skirt) is the direct object (accusative masculine: ein→einen).

sections.title: Putting It Together
sections.explanation: To determine the case of a noun, ask these questions: Who/what performs the action? → Nominative (subject). Who/what receives the action directly? → Accusative (direct object). In the sentence 'Der Hund beißt den Mann' (The dog bites the man): 'der Hund' is the subject (nominative — performing the biting), 'den Mann' is the direct object (accusative — receiving the bite). Note that 'den' (not 'der') signals 'Mann' is the object — this is how German signals meaning even when word order changes.
sections.tip: Nominative asks: WHO is doing it? Accusative asks: WHAT/WHO is being acted upon? The 'den' signals the object.

examples.en: The dog bites the man. (Dog=nom., Man=acc.)
The man is bitten by the dog. (same meaning! Cases show roles)
A child sees a cat. (beide: Kind=nom., Katze=acc. fem. — no change)

exercises.prompt: In 'Der Hund beißt den Mann', who is the subject?
exercises.explanation: 'Der Hund' (nominative) is the subject doing the biting. 'Den Mann' (accusative, masculine) is the object being bitten.

exercises.prompt: Why can German say 'Den Mann beißt der Hund' and it still means the dog bites the man?
options: Because German word order is always fixed
Because the articles show the case — den (acc.) = object, der (nom.) = subject
exercises.explanation: German uses cases (articles/endings) to show who does what, not just word order. 'den' marks the man as the object regardless of where it appears.

exercises.prompt: Ich sehe ___ Lehrer. (I see the teacher.) — Lehrer is masculine
exercises.explanation: Masculine direct object: der→den. 'Ich sehe den Lehrer' = I see the teacher.

exercises.prompt: Which article form signals 'direct object, masculine'?
exercises.explanation: 'den' (definite) and 'einen' (indefinite) both signal masculine accusative — i.e., a masculine direct object.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Sie kauft ___ Rock und ___ Bluse.' (She buys a skirt and a blouse. Rock=masc., Bluse=fem.)
exercises.explanation: Rock (masc.) → einen Rock (acc. indef. masc.). Bluse (fem.) → eine Bluse (acc. indef. fem. — no change). 'Sie kauft einen Rock und eine Bluse.'
